coredata.dc:title: Nanoscale carbon quantum dots activate antiviral immune responses and modulate the heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoprotein interface by inducing differential alternative splicing
coredata.dc:creator: Farooq, Tahir
Hussain, Muhammad Dilshad
Liu, Dandan
Kamran, Ali
He, Zifu
Tang, Yafei
She, Xiaoman
coredata.dc:description: Plant viruses threaten global food security by subverting host defenses, necessitating sustainable solutions. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-nanoscale materials with tunable optoelectronic properties and biocompatibility, offer promise in antiviral therapy, but their mechanisms in plant immunity remain unclear. This study comprehensively mapped CQDs-modulated transcriptional reprogramming of antiviral responses in association with the diversity and patterns of alternative splicing (AS) events in cotton leaf curl Multan virus CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected Nicotiana benthamiana. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that at 5 days post-inoculation (dpi) with CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β), the CQDs upregulated antiviral immunity-related genes (Ca+2/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase (CaMK), WRKY transcription factors, mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), and serine/threonine protein kinases (STPKs) and stress-responsive immunity pathways. At 20 dpi, the transcriptional response shifted to reflect reduced virus-induced stress. Moreover, the CQDs differentially impacted AS patterns by increasing exon skipping and modulating intron retention, mainly at later stages of viral infection. Structural modeling revealed that the CQDs induced conformational changes in the RRM domain-containing heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoprotein (hnRNP), enhancing protein druggability and binding capacity. These findings indicate the dynamic role of CQDs in the modulation of antiviral immune responses and the transcriptomic plasticity of plants during viral infection which is reflected by regulation of gene expression and alternative splicing. CQDs showed strong antiviral potential in alleviating symptoms of CLCuMuV via reprogramming transcriptional responses and differential AS events in N. benthamiana. These findings underscore CQDs as effective tools for enhancing plant resilience against viral pathogens. Future research should explore field efficacy of CQDs and their nanoscale interactions with viral proteins and their broader applications in sustainable crop protection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of RNA-seq data and AS profiling in the N. benthamiana plants. Representative images corresponding to different treatments including (A) healthy, (B) CQDs-treated, (C), CQDs-treated + CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected and (D) CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected plants at 20 dpi. (E) Steps for RNA-seq analysis. RNA from plants from above-mentioned groups was extracted at 5 and 20 dpi and subjected to Illumina sequencing. (F) HISAT2 was employed for rapid and highly accurate spliced alignment of QC sequences against reference genome. (G) Expression density plots corresponding to CLCuMuV (A + β)-infected plants with and without CQDs treatment at 5 and 20 dpi. The insets show the expression density differences among the samples. (H) Expression density boxplots of the treatments mentioned earlier at 5 and 20 dpi. (I) Workflow of alternative splicing analysis of hnRNP and other immunity-related genes.

CQDs-induced antiviral immune responses in virus-infected N. benthamiana. (A) Expression profiles of antiviral immunity-related genes at 5 dpi. (B) CQDs-induced expression of key genes involved in antiviral immunity. (C) Nucleotide sequence encoding a Ca2+/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase with highlighted active binding sites and conserved domains. (D-E) Expression profiles and log2-fold change values of the key CQDs-induced genes associated with antiviral immunity in CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected N. benthamiana at 20 dpi. (F) Nucleotide sequence of an important CQDs-induced calmodulin-like protein along with highlighted conserved domains.

Comparison of differential alternative splicing events. (A-B) AS events were detected in healthy (CN), virus-infected (CP) and virus-infected+CQDs-treated (QV) N. benthamiana at 5 dpi. (C–D) Analysis of AS events among the treatments mentioned above at 20 dpi. AS analysis was performed using SplAdder (Kahles et al., 2016) (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26873928/).

Alternative splicing events and transcript profiles across various treatments. (A) The exon–intron structures and specific AS patterns were analyzed by mapping the treatment-based sequences against the gene locus “Niben261Chr14g1271011.1” located on chromosome 14. The precise location of AS events is detailed by the genomic coordinates (ranging from 127,124 kb to 127,132 kb). (B–C) Differential expression patterns of transcripts showing splicing isoform abundance across different treatments. CN, CP and QV correspond to healthy/uninfected, CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected and CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected+CQDs-treated plants, respectively, at 5 and 20 dpi.

Alternative splicing patterns across different conditions. (A) Sashimi plot illustrating AS patterns among the CN5, CP5, QV5, CN20, CP20, and QV20 treatments. Each track represents RNA-seq read coverage, showing variations in exon inclusion or skipping within a genomic region. The numbers (e.g., 264, 580) indicate junction support for specific splicing events. Ovals encircle splice junctions, suggesting differential inclusion or exclusion of exons or splice sites. The genomic scale at the bottom pinpoints the analyzed region corresponding to Niben261Chr14g1271011.1. (B-G) Individual and comparative estimation of the percentages of alternatively spliced exons and the corresponding mean differences across various treatments.

CQDs-induced modulation of the RRM interface and binding capacity. Comparison of RRM-associated (A) nucleotide and (B) amino acid sequences. The dashed rectangles and red stars highlight the CQDs-modulated insertions. (C) 3D structure-based modeling of the RRM protein associated with healthy and virus-infected plants. (D) CQDs-mediated modulation of the RRM C-terminus. The letters N and C denote the start and end of the polypeptide chain, respectively. (E-F) Analysis of the binding pockets associated with the RRM structure. Å3, Å2 and ds correspond to the volume, surface and drug score/druggability of the protein, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
List of antiviral immunity/defense-related genes induced by CQDs treatment of CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected N. benthamiana at 5 dpi.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plant viruses threaten global food security by subverting host defenses, necessitating sustainable solutions. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-nanoscale materials with tunable optoelectronic properties and biocompatibility, offer promise in antiviral therapy, but their mechanisms in plant immunity remain unclear. This study comprehensively mapped CQDs-modulated transcriptional reprogramming of antiviral responses in association with the diversity and patterns of alternative splicing (AS) events in cotton leaf curl Multan virus CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected Nicotiana benthamiana. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that at 5 days post-inoculation (dpi) with CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β), the CQDs upregulated antiviral immunity-related genes (Ca+2/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase (CaMK), WRKY transcription factors, mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), and serine/threonine protein kinases (STPKs) and stress-responsive immunity pathways. At 20 dpi, the transcriptional response shifted to reflect reduced virus-induced stress. Moreover, the CQDs differentially impacted AS patterns by increasing exon skipping and modulating intron retention, mainly at later stages of viral infection. Structural modeling revealed that the CQDs induced conformational changes in the RRM domain-containing heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoprotein (hnRNP), enhancing protein druggability and binding capacity. These findings indicate the dynamic role of CQDs in the modulation of antiviral immune responses and the transcriptomic plasticity of plants during viral infection which is reflected by regulation of gene expression and alternative splicing. CQDs showed strong antiviral potential in alleviating symptoms of CLCuMuV via reprogramming transcriptional responses and differential AS events in N. benthamiana. These findings underscore CQDs as effective tools for enhancing plant resilience against viral pathogens. Future research should explore field efficacy of CQDs and their nanoscale interactions with viral proteins and their broader applications in sustainable crop protection.

Nanoscale carbon quantum dots activate antiviral immunity via AS-triggered modulation of hnRNP.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets presented in this study can be found online by accessing the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI), BioProject number PRJNA1155227.

ce:section.ce:para: Like nefarious invaders, plant viruses breach host defenses to enter the cell and fight against cellular immune responses, causing infectious diseases and leading to a quarter of food production losses, thereby threatening global food security (Rivarez et al., 2023; Schulz et al., 2020). To counteract viral infection, plants have evolved multilayered defense strategies involving incompatible interactions, activating the expression of antiviral immunity-related genes involved in RNA silencing, pattern-triggered immunity (PTI), and effector-triggered immunity (ETI), as well as triggering the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), nitric oxide (NO), phytohormone signaling [salicylic acid (SA), jasmonate (JA), ethylene (ET)], and hypersensitive response (HR) (Leonetti et al., 2021; Snoeck et al., 2024; Staiger et al., 2013; Zipfel and Oldroyd, 2017). For example, host R (resistance) gene products and nucleotide binding site leucine-rich repeats (CC-NBS-LRR and TIR-NBS-LRR) recognize and interact with several viral proteins (effectors) (Sett et al., 2022), such as AC4 of tomato leaf curl New Delhi virus (ToLCNDV) (Sharma et al., 2021), replication-associated protein (Rep/C1) of tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV) (Yamaguchi et al., 2018), coat protein (CP) and RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (Nib) of potato virus Y (PVY) (Grech-Baran et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2017), p50 of tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) (Ueda et al., 2006), cell–cell movement protein (NSM) and silencing suppressor (NSS) of tomato spotted wilt virus (TSWV) (De Ronde et al., 2013; Hallwass et al., 2014), and P38 of turnip crinkle virus (TCV) (Chandra-Shekara et al., 2004), induce ETI, resulting in HR. Furthermore, HR promotes the biosynthesis of mobile immune regulators (azelaic acid (AzA), glycerol-3-phosphate (G3P), and methyl salicylic acid (MeSA)), which translocate systemically and induce the accumulation of salicylic acid (SA) and systemic acquired resistance (SAR) (Gao et al., 2021; Han, 2019; Riedlmeier et al., 2017). Strikingly, these NBS-LRR genes need to be tightly regulated because, in the absence of viral infection, NBS-LRR genes can also trigger autoimmunity, resulting in a “growth–defense trade-off” (Chae et al., 2014; Karasov et al., 2017; Ordon et al., 2021). Moreover, the exact downstream signaling mechanisms after the recognition of effector molecules require mechanistic exploration. Besides agriculture, the application of nanoparticles has been well documented in the biomedical sciences where they have been reported to interact with a wide range of biological systems resulting in the positive regulation of antioxidant enzymes including catalase, superoxide dismutase and malondialdehyde (Rovshan et al., 2024). Due to their excellent properties, nanomaterials, especially those synthesized from natural resources are being used for cancer therapy (Montazersaheb et al., 2024; Nasibova et al., 2024).

Nevertheless, given the constant evolutionary pressure between plants and pathogens for dominance, it is generally accepted that several of the defensive mechanisms of plants may be masked by the coevolving immune-suppressing functions of pathogens. In fact, alternative splicing (AS), a posttranscriptional mechanism that regulates mRNA levels and increases transcriptome complexity and thereby the proteome diversity of a cell, has long been recognized to contribute significantly to plant immunity (Godinho et al., 2024; Jordan et al., 2002). Interestingly, several alternative splicing events have been reported in response to biotic stress (Huang et al., 2017; Mandadi and Scholthof, 2015; Tong et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2019) as well as different abiotic factors (Laloum et al., 2018; Staiger and Brown, 2013). Upon pathogen infection, many genes with key pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) and nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat receptors (NLRs) involved in PTI and ETI undergo AS and express alternative isoforms implicated in defensive functions (Du et al., 2024; Huang et al., 2021; Kufel et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2024; Rigo et al., 2019; Su et al., 2023; Zuo et al., 2022). For example, the AS of the tobacco N gene is essential for immunity against TMV (Dinesh and Baker, 2000), and the AS of the tomato translation initiation factor eIF4E is essential for the resistance response against potyviruses (Piron et al., 2010). Thus, hundreds of genes affecting metabolism, signal transduction, transcriptional regulation, posttranscriptional gene silencing, protein degradation, and other cellular processes are modulated in both compatible and incompatible plant–virus interactions (Mandadi and Scholthof, 2015).

During viral infection, host plants possess dynamic AS patterns that induce transcript isoform switches and thereby alter protein function, localization, or stability. For instance, AS of resistance (R) genes often produces isoforms with full-length or truncated proteins that are able to differentially modulate immune signaling or prevent autoimmunity under non-infected conditions. In addition, the splicing factors themselves are controlled by pathogen-responsive signaling, which suggests feedback regulation in the splicing apparatus (Staiger and Brown, 2013). Host splicing machinery is also utilized by viruses to preferentially use isoforms that subvert plant immunity, reflecting an arms race between plant hosts and pathogens via co-evolution (Dinesh and Baker, 2000). Key regulators such as serine/arginine-rich (SR) proteins are also revealed to be differentially phosphorylated and expressed upon pathogen infection, and they influence the splicing of defense-related transcripts. Moreover, AS plays a role in hormonal crosstalk during defense, i.e., regulation of signaling pathways involving salicylic acid, jasmonic acid, and ethylene, which are at the center of plant immunity (Godinho et al., 2024). The integration of AS into immune signaling allows plants to respond to quickly modify their defense repertoire in response to various and dynamic pathogenic threats, demonstrating the biological significance of AS in constructing hardy and nimble immune responses (Godinho et al., 2024).

Nanomaterials, such as carboned based quantum dots (CQDs), have been proved to be powerful tools forplant protection owing to their distinctive physicochemical properties, such as finely tunable surface chemistry, superior biocompatibility and the ability to regulate oxidative stress pathways (Ahmed et al., 2024; Łoczechin et al., 2019). Engineered nanoparticles such as silver, zinc oxide and silica have shown anti-viral effect bydirect virus inactivation, improvement of ROS scavenging or induction of systemic acquired resistance (Adeel et al., 2021; Cai et al., 2019). In recent decades, several reports have indicated that engineered nanoparticles (NPs) disrupt such plant–virus interactions in favor of plant immunity, consequently inhibiting virus infection (Adeel et al., 2021; Alkubaisi and Aref, 2017; Cai et al., 2019; Hao et al., 2018; Noha et al., 2018). CQDs, as a group of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, have shown great potential owing to their low toxicity, photostability andability to interact with biomolecules such as nucleic acids and proteins through their surface functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl) (Chen et al., 2022; Kumar et al., 2022). Recent reports suggest that CQDs induce salicylic acid dependent defense responses and repress viral replication via the repression of RNA silencing suppressors of thevirus (Farooq et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2024). In our recent study, we reported that infection with cotton leaf curl Multan virus (CLCuMuV; genus Begomovirus, family Geminiviridae) disrupted plant immunity, whereas CQDs enhanced the antiviral response by regulating major defense-related genes associated with endocytosis/necroptosis, Tam3-transposase, ABC transporters/sphingolipid signaling pathway, and serine/threonine protein kinase (STPKs) activities. Furthermore, the CQDs potentially triggered TSS and TTS events in CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected plants (Farooq et al., 2024). However, the genome-wide transcriptional profiles of host and CLCuMuV transcripts mechanistically involved in AS events, which induce antiviral immune responses during host–virus interactions, have not been fully explored. Therefore, the objective of this study was to investigate the CQDs-induced transcriptional reprogramming of the plant's antiviral immunity and to uncover a comprehensive landscape of CQDs-regulated AS events and diversity in the virus-infected plants, eventually contributing to sustainable disease management.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plant growth conditions, virus source, and agroinoculation
ce:section.ce:para: Nicotiana benthamiana wild-type (WT) plants were grown in substrate media consisting of vermiculite, perlite, black soil, and artificial soil (2:2:2:1). The plants were maintained at ∼60 % relative humidity (RH) under a photoperiod of 14 h light and 10 h dark, and the temperature ranged from 25 to 27 °C. These N. benthamiana plants, at the 5–7 fully expanded leaf stage, were agroinfiltrated with CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β). Agrobacterium tumefaciens (strain GV3101) harboring CLCuMuV DNA-A (KP762786) and its associated betasatellite (KP762787) infectious clones were cultured overnight in LB liquid broth with appropriate antibiotics until they reached an OD600 = 1. The overnight-cultured bacterial constructs carrying CLCuMuV DNA-A and betasatellite were suspended in agroinfiltration buffer (MMA; 10 mM MgCl2, 10 mM MES pH 5.6, and 150 μM Acetosyringone) at a 1:1 ratio and used to infiltrate the lower epidermis of N. benthamiana plants via a 1 ml needleless syringe. The plants were monitored regularly for the development of symptoms, and the presence of CLCuMuV was confirmed with RT–PCR assay using a specific pair of primers, namely, CLCuMuV-CL F/R & CLCuMuB-betaF/R (Supplementary Table 1). In all the experiments, CN represents the untreated negative/healthy control, CP represents the virus-infected positive control, QD represents the CQDs-treated, virus-free plants, and QV represents the virus-infected and CQDs-treated N. benthamiana plants. “CLCuMuV” represents coinfection by DNA-A + betasatellite unless otherwise specified, and the numbers 5 and 20 represent the days post inoculation (dpi).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization and application of fluorescent CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent, water-soluble CQDs were synthesized via hydrothermal processing with malic acid via a previously described method (Farooq et al., 2024). Furthermore, for the molecular and systematic characterization of the synthesized CQDs, their physiochemical properties were analyzed through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (FEI, Talos F200X). To determine the particle size, crystal structure, and phase purity of the CQDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted via a Bruker D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (40 mA, 40 kV, 10° -90°, 5°/min). To identify the presence of functional groups associated with the CQDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum analysis was also performed via a Nicolet iS 10 FTIR spectrometer with a spectral range of 400–4000 cm−1. Additionally, quantitative spectroscopic measurements were taken via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI instrument for the confirmation of the FT-IR assignments. The XPS-specific parameters [monochromatic Al Kα (hv = 1486.6 eV) with a power of 150 W, 650 μm beam spot, 14.8 kV voltage, 1.6 A current, and contaminated carbon C1s = 284.8 eV calibration for potential corrections] were adjusted for the confirmation of the FT-IR spectrum analysis. The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the CQDs was characterized via an Edinburgh FLS980 instrument, and the absorption spectra were analyzed via a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV–2600).

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA extraction, cDNA synthesis, and RT–qPCR
ce:section.ce:para: The samples were harvested, and total RNA was extracted from 100 mg of freeze-dried leaves via TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen, Life Technologies, Carlsbad, CA, USA) following the manufacturer's instructions. The quality and quantity of the RNA were determined via a NanoDrop spectrophotometer (ND-1000, Fisher Thermo, Wilmington, DE, USA). These extracted RNAs were subsequently reverse- transcribed into complementary DNAs (cDNAs) with Moloney murine leukemia virus reverse transcriptase (MMuLV-RT) (Sigma Aldrich, USA). To confirm the mRNA expression profiles of the candidate genes, reverse transcription–quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT–qPCR) was conducted via TB Green® Premix Ex Taq™ II (Tli RNaseHPlus) (Takara Bio, Inc.) according to the manufacturer's instructions. To calculate the relative mRNA expression, the 2-ΔΔCT method was used, as previously reported (Livak and Schmittgen, 2001). Each treatment included a total of three biological and nine technical replicates. The primers used for RT–qPCR analysis are listed in Supplementary Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: cDNA library preparation, RNA-seq, and bioinformatics analyses
ce:section.ce:para: To construct paired-end cDNA libraries, approximately 1.5 μg of RNA from each sample was processed and employed to generate cDNA libraries via the ABclonal mRNA-seq Lib Prep Kit (ABclonal, China) following the manufacturer's guidelines, and the resulting products were purified using the AMPure XP system according to the provided protocol. The quality and integrity of the purified products were determined with an Agilent Bioanalyzer 4150 system. The sequencing of these purified products was conducted in paired-end mode via the NovaSeq 6000/MGISEQ-T7 sequencing platform (Illumina BGI, China). Low-quality reads, as well as those with adapters or poly-N sequences, were subsequently removed to obtain clean data. The data were filtered to acquire high-quality, paired-end reads, which were aligned to the N. benthamiana reference genome (Niben-V.261). Then, HTSEq. (v0.6.1) was employed in Python to count the number of reads mapped to N. benthamiana genes. The expected values of the transcripts per million (TPM) and fragments per kilobase per million mapped fragments (FPKM) were obtained by analyzing the fraction of transcripts/cells and estimating the read counts mapped to the target gene and the relative gene length, respectively. The DESeq2 package was employed in R to analyze and compare differential gene expression (Love et al., 2014).

For identification, differential analysis and quantification of AS events between different samples, we employed the SplAdder (3.1.0) algorithm as described previously (Kahles et al., 2016). Briefly, the RNA-seq data from different samples were annotated into splicing graphs, followed by augmentation of those graphs with AS evidence. The enriched graphs were subsequently processed to identify splicing events, followed by differential analysis. The source code of SplAdder is available on GitHub (http://github.com/ratschlab/spladder). Additional methodology associated with detection of AS events from untreated, CQDs-treated, and virus-infected plants are outlined in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: hnRNP homology modeling and druggability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: For multiple (nucleotide and amino acid) sequence alignments, Geneious R9 (version 9.0.2) was used (Kearse et al., 2012). For sequence analysis and identification of the conserved domains and functional/binding sites, the Conserved Domains Database (CDD) was employed (Wang et al., 2023). The 3D protein structure was predicted via the I-TASSER server as described previously (Zheng et al., 2021). Additional structural confirmation and analysis of the binding pockets were performed via PyMol software (version 2.5.2).

The raw sequence data associated with this study can be found in the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) database under the following SRA accession numbers: SRX25963836, SRX25963837, SRX25963838, SRX25963839, SRX25963840, SRX25963841, SRX25963842, and SRX25963843. The corresponding BioProject accession number is PRJNA1155227.

We used false discovery rate (FDR) for multiple testing and the cutoff FC values (≥ 2 and ≤ −2) were considered to identify the differentially expressed genes with significance (adjusted P value < 0.05). The inter-group data variability was investigated by employing principal component analysis (PCA) whereas, the significant variability was identified via analysis of similarity (ANOSIM) test. Furthermore, Bray-Curtis (statistical algorithm in R's Vegan package) was employed to perform hierarchical cluster analysis. For SplAdder-based analysis of alternative splicing, negative bionomial distribution was used to model count data; GLM-based likelihood ratio test to detect differential usage between treatments and Benjamini-Hochberg FDR was used for corrected multiple testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Landscape of early- and late-stage CQDs-modulated antiviral immunity
CQDs-mediated differential AS in CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected N. benthamiana
Isoform-level comparison of AS events in heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoprotein
ce:section.ce:para: Although our preliminary AS analysis revealed several genes associated with differential AS in the CQDs-treated N. benthamiana, owing to the significant DPSI score, we selected an RRM domain-containing heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoprotein (hnRNP) for further analysis (Supplementary Fig. 2). To illustrate different AS events across various treatments (CN5, CP5, QV5, CN20, CP20, and QV20), the RNA sequence was mapped to Niben261Chr14g1271011.1. At 5 dpi, the splicing pattern exhibited several short-range arcs connecting adjacent exons, indicating canonical intron splicing in QV5. However, the overall splicing activity appeared moderate, suggesting typical splicing at 5 dpi. Moreover, an even distribution of histograms reflected balanced transcription (Fig. 4A). Nevertheless, compared with CP5, QV5 appeared to have more defined arcs, indicating a greater number/diversity of AS events (Fig. 4A). At a later stage (20 dpi), the CQDs-treated plants infected with CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β) (QV20) presented more complex and long-range arcs connecting distant exons than did those infected with QV5. This suggested that the QV shifted/enhanced AS processing at 20 dpi. Additionally, while the overall exon read coverage remained consistent, the splicing complexity in QV20 increased markedly (Fig. 4A). On the other hand, the healthy (CN20) and CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected (CP20) groups presented relatively simple splicing patterns with few long-range arcs and primarily short-range canonical splicing events. This finding also implied that the CQDs uniquely drove increased exon–exon interactions and AS patterns at 20 dpi (Fig. 4A). When tested statistically, we found that the CQDs-driven splicing complexity significantly differed from that of the CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected plants without CQDs treatment (Fig. 4B-C). Taken together, these findings suggest that CQDs application, specifically at 20 dpi, led to markedly greater splicing complexity in the CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected plants than in the virus-infected plants without CQDs treatment (CP20).

We further analyzed data to investigate the effects of different treatments on AS patterns, particularly by comparing the usage of transcript isoforms and the proportion of alternatively spliced isoforms at 5 and 20 dpi. The results revealed that at 5 dpi, the AS of QV5 was lower than that of CP5, as indicated by fewer and less prominent arcs. This indicated that the CQDs had a smaller effect on splicing activity than did the virus-infected plants (CP5) (Fig. 5A). However, at 20 dpi, QV20 presented a higher AS rate than did CP20. The arcs in QV20 indicated greater utilization of certain splice junctions, which implies that the CQDs exert a more significant regulatory influence on splicing at 20 dpi (Fig. 5A). We next compared the abundance of alternatively spliced isoforms between different treatments at 5 dpi. The findings revealed that at 5 dpi, CQDs had a slightly greater percentage of alternatively spliced isoforms than did the healthy group (CN5), but this difference was not statistically significant (Fig. 5B). On the other hand, the virus-infected group (CP5) had a remarkably greater percentage of alternatively spliced isoforms than did the QV5 group, indicating that virus infection alone (CP5) more strongly induced AS than the QV5 group at 5 dpi (Fig. 5C-D).

Notably, the AS of QV20 was obviously greater than that of CN20, which indicated that the application of the CQDs to the CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected plants strongly influenced splicing regulation at 20 dpi (Fig. 5E). While CP20 presented higher splicing levels compared to CN20 (Fig. 5F), a comparison with the CQDs-treated group demonstrated that, compared with CP20, QV20 presented slightly increased splicing activity (Fig. 5G). This finding indicates that at the later stage, the CQDs have a slightly stronger effect on splicing pathways in the presence of the virus. In summary, these results show a temporal shift in the effect of the CQDs on the AS. Compared with those of the virus-infected plants (CP5), the splicing activity of the CQDs at the early stage of virus infection (5 dpi) was less affected, as reflected by the lower usage of splice junctions and lower percentages of alternatively spliced isoforms. However, at the later stage (20 dpi), CQDs application strongly increased the splicing activity, which was much greater than that of healthy (CN20) and only virus-infected (CP20) plants. These results highlight that the CQDs could exert late but more dramatic regulatory effects on splicing pathways, possibly due to gradual changes in gene expression over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs modulate the RRM interface, modify the structure, and enhance its binding capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Given the significant effect of the CQDs on the AS pattern and isoform diversity at 20 dpi, we sought to further analyze how the CQDs affect the structure and possibly modify the RRM domain-containing hnRNP. A comparison of nucleotide sequences revealed that CLCuMuV infection alone did not alter the sequence; however, the CQDs-associated variant (QV20) lacked the seven-nucleotide sequence “CCAGCAG”, which resulted in a frameshift (Fig. 6A). Consequently, the amino acid sequence corresponding to the CQDs-treated group exhibited an altered sequence containing “EFRH” amino acids, encoding a relatively shorter protein (lacking “RRYRPY”) as compared to the healthy (CN20) and CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected (CP20) groups (Fig. 6B). Additional analysis involving the construction and comparison of the 3D structures of the healthy/CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected proteins with those of the CQDs-treated proteins revealed that the primary protein structure underwent a conformational shift upon interaction with the CQDs (Fig. 6C-D). This implies that CQDs can potentially induce modulation of the C-terminal region of the RRM interface, which might lead to altered protein functions, including RNA binding capacity, among others. To further test whether this CQDs-influenced modulation of the protein structure affects its binding capacity, we quantified and compared the protein surface and volumetric changes. Notably, the CQDs-induced structural shifts significantly affected the binding interfaces, resulting in markedly increased values of volume, surface area, and drug score/druggability from 642.04 (Å3), 1133.19 (Å2) and 0.78 (ds) to 856.22 (Å3), 1415.61 (Å2) and 0.83 (ds), respectively (Fig. 6E-F). Taken together, these results showcase the conformational impact of CQDs on the RRM interface of hnRNP, where they can induce structural rearrangements of the C-terminal region, resulting in alterations in the solvent-accessible surface area and volume, which might consequently affect protein function.

Recently, CQDs have attracted much attention because of their excellent physicochemical properties, such as fluorescence, biocompatibility, and chemical stability, which make them versatile for applications in fields ranging from bioimaging and drug delivery to disease control (Łoczechin et al., 2019; Pourmadadi et al., 2023). In addition to their applications against human/animal viruses (Xue et al., 2022), CQDs hold great potential as efficient alternatives for controlling plant viral infections, where traditional methods are often still hampered by poor efficacy, scalability, or sustainability. CLCuMuV is a member of the genus Begomovirus that severely threatens cotton cultivation and other crop production systems because of its massive economic impact (Farooq et al., 2021; Qadir et al., 2019). The limited effectiveness of the currently available disease management approaches suggests the need for continuous planning, testing, and implementation of novel strategies. In this work, the antiviral activity of CQDs was studied in N. benthamiana plants infected with CLCuMuV to demonstrate how CQDs affect the host plant transcriptomic landscape, modulate immune responses, and induce alternative splicing-mediated changes at the protein level, revealing a multifaceted mechanistic strategy for sustainable viral disease management. These findings present broader implications for CQDs in both plant virology and nanobiotechnology.

The detailed morphological and structural investigation of the CQDs (Supplementary Fig. 3) revealed their spherical shape with a narrow size distribution, with an average diameter of 5.827 nm [45]. In addition, FFT analysis confirmed their crystalline structure with a hexagonal lattice. This uniformity provides consistent performance and enhances their usefulness in biological applications (Das et al., 2024). The functional groups endow CQDs with excellent hydrophilicity and stability, two important properties for their biological compatibility (Kumar et al., 2022). Moreover, the very high quantum yield and fluorescence emission peak at 445 nm further confirmed the suitability of CQDs for applications requiring accurate tracking within biological environments (Kumar et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2019). The structural features of CQDs play a critical role in their ability to interact with biological species, including nucleic acids and proteins (Chen et al., 2022; Zare et al., 2021). This interaction forms the basis for their antiviral activity. More studies are therefore needed to establish how variations in the synthesis of CQDs (e.g., starting materials and reaction conditions) affect their activity and stability under different environmental conditions.

Compared with the untreated control, the CQDs effectively promoted antiviral activity in the CLCuMuV (DNA-A + β)-infected N. benthamiana plants, in which the treated plants showed fewer severe viral symptoms and lower levels of viral transcripts. Visible effects were apparent during the early (5 dpi) and late stages (20 dpi) of infection but generally preserved the health and physiological function of the CQDs-treated plants (Farooq et al., 2024). The modulation of physiological activities like photosynthesis and other signaling molecules is another mechanism by which CQDs regulate plant stress responses and enhance immunity (Gong and Zhao, 2018). This ability to “prime” the host plant for increased resistance to infection by a virus would be an even more intriguing suggestion for the pre-infection application of CQDs. CQDs-mediated activation of immune signaling pathways, inhibition of viral replication, and direct interactions with viral particles (Chen et al., 2022; Fu et al., 2024; Huang et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2022) might contribute to such priming. Furthermore, CQDs have been reported to enhance the activities of antioxidant enzymes such as catalase (CAT) and superoxide dismutase (SOD) which indicates that CQDs can activate oxidative stress responses, which are often linked with enhanced plant immunity (Li et al., 2019). Prophylactic applications hold promise, but their large-scale utilization remains challenging. In combination with other antiviral agents, further exploration in post-infection applications can broaden the usefulness of CQDs. A very important finding was the long-lasting antiviral activity of the CQDs (up to 20 dpi). Indeed, such durability is in opposition to traditional chemical-based treatments, which may require repeated applications and result in harmful environmental effects in some cases. However, the dose and application frequency of CQDs still need to be further optimized under different environmental conditions for wider adoption.

Transcriptomic analysis revealed a dramatic temporal alteration in the host gene expression profile as a consequence of CQD treatment. Specifically, at 5 dpi, genes involved in stress and immune responses, such as WRKY transcription factors, serine/threonine kinases, and calmodulin-dependent proteins, were highly upregulated. These proteins are integral components of pathways such as MAPK cascades and calcium-mediated signaling, which feature prominently in antiviral defense mechanisms (Zhang and Klessig, 2001; Zvereva et al., 2023). By 20 dpi, the transcriptomic landscape changed, with fewer differentially expressed genes. This could indicate normalization of the stress responses, possibly due to the resolution of viral stress or an adaptive steady-state response mediated by the CQDs. However, some immune-related genes involved in synthesizing NADPH oxidases and secondary metabolites were still active at this stage, indicating sustained immune readiness. Recently, researchers have highlighted the involvement of quantum dots and other engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) in the modulation plant's immune responses (Ahmed et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024). Specifically, ENMs can be employed as efficient nanocarriers for precise delivery of genes and/or immune signaling molecules in the cells to enhance plant immunity to improve disease resistance (Zhang et al., 2021).

The temporal variation in the transcriptomic responses underscores the multifaceted role of the CQDs. Early in infection, an increased immune response might prevent viral replication or spread (Mueller and Rouse, 2008; Paludan et al., 2024). Later, this balance may be normalized to allow growth at the expense of minimizing the metabolic costs associated with prolonged immune activation (Palmer, 2022). This adaptability reflects an efficient host–pathogen interaction modulated by the CQDs. Further studies using methods such as coexpression network analysis or proteomics could increase the complexity of the regulatory mechanisms in which CQDs are involved. Identification of the principal regulators or central hub genes affected by CQDs would offer more mechanistic insights into their antiviral properties.

AS is a key player in plant immunity, increasing transcript diversity and enabling rapid adaptation to stress (Filichkin et al., 2015; Rigo et al., 2019). CQDs treatment triggered significant AS changes, where intron retention was prevalent at 5 dpi and exon skipping became more predominant at 20 dpi. This temporal in the distinct splicing patterns indicates that the CQDs influence the plant RNA processing machinery in a time-dependent manner. In particular, the modulation of hnRNP (hnRNP) splicing by CQDs was striking. hnRNPs are central regulators of RNA metabolism in the regulation of splicing, transport, and stability (Dreyfuss et al., 1993; Geuens et al., 2016; Han et al., 2010). Altered hnRNP structure and splicing patterns can increase a plant's ability to synthesize defense-related proteins or inhibit viral RNA processing, rendering the virus incapable of replication (Bhattarai and Holcik, 2022). Critically, the interaction between the changes in transcriptomics and splicing highlights the dual regulatory role of the CQDs. Thus, CQDs amplify the host antiviral response by simultaneously influencing gene expression and posttranscriptional processing. Despite the fact that nanomaterials enhance plant immunity against viruses, a direct interaction between nanomaterials and the viral pathogen might also lead to the viral inactivation. This has been demonstrated in a recent study where researchers reported that TMV was inactivated via electrostatic interaction between TMV coat protein and star polycation (SPc) nanocarrier (Jiang et al., 2024). These effects, if explored in other plant–virus systems, may support the use of cement CQDs as a universal tool for managing viral infections. The structural changes induced by the CQDs in the hnRNPs offered a plausible mechanism for their antiviral activity (Maceratessi and Sampaio, 2024). Specifically, treatment with CQD resulted in the skipping of a seven-nucleotide sequence, which induced a frameshift and changed the conformation of hnRNP. This conformational change increased the binding surface area and druggability of the protein, increasing its ability to regulate RNA metabolism under stress conditions. Given that hnRNP is an RRM domain-containing protein (Geuens et al., 2016), an alternatively spliced motif could result in the recognition of a different motif during its biological interactions. Although we focused only on one alternatively spliced gene (hnRNP) that plays a role in the antiviral immune system, the remaining identified genes are equivalently important. Given the challenges associated with annotation and the high-quality N. benthamiana reference genome, some of the alternatively spliced genes might not seem to be involved but could actually play an important role in antiviral immunity.

These observations further support the assumption that the action of CQDs is not limited to only the transcriptional level (Lin et al., 2023) but also directly affects protein function (Kunachowicz et al., 2022). Such alterations may disturb the interactions of viral proteins or increase the activity of host proteins. Besides molecular docking and simulation, additional studies involving the use of fluorescent or circular dichroism spectroscopy, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, isothermal titration calorimetry, surface plasmon resonance, dynamic light scattering and zeta potential, atomic force microscopy, and modified electrophoretic mobility shift assay can be performed to investigate the CQDs and RRM protein interactions. Notably, the long-term toxicity data analysis and inclusion of field-based experiment would further strengthen the research findings. Using detailed explorations based on molecular dynamics simulations would offer insight into the detailed interactions occurring between CQDs and their protein targets.

This study demonstrates the potential of CQDs as antiviral agents with implications for plant virology and nanotechnology. The findings presented here open several important questions and avenues for future research. For example, advanced imaging and structural biology techniques could help to provide mechanistic clarity regarding how CQDs interact specifically with viral particles and host molecules. Can CQDs be developed for other complex plant–virus systems or even human pathogens? Cross-species efficacy studies would dramatically expand their utility. Importantly, knowledge of the stability, persistence, and scalability of CQDs in diverse agricultural scenarios is critically important before further practical implementation can be undertaken. Owing to their ability to affect gene expression, alternative splicing, and protein function, CQDs offer a wide toolkit for fighting viral infections. They are also nontoxic and environmentally friendly (Kasirajan et al., 2024), making them a sustainable alternative to traditional disease control approaches. Due to the quick development of nanomaterials in agriculture, there is an immediate need to assess their biosafety and environmental impact. Especially, by focusing on carbon quantum dots' environmentally friendly nature and considering their future fate in aquatic environments and soils would provide more ecological outreach for future investigations.

This work employs CQDs as novel antiviral therapeutic agents for plants, with a focus on the modulation of transcriptional regulation, effects on AS, and interaction with proteins. The CQDs exhibited high monodispersity, a spherical morphology, and nanoscale dimensions. CQDs modulated transcriptional reprogramming by inducing stress-related pathways and stabilized plant responses over time. Key defense-related genes, including calcium-binding proteins and WRKY transcription factors, were significantly upregulated, indicating the activation of antiviral pathways. Furthermore, the CQDs markedly increased AS activity, with increased utilization of specific splice junctions and greater isoform diversity, which implies that the CQDs progressively impact splicing regulation, aligning with their role in transcriptomic stabilization and defense activation. Additionally, they cause a conformational change in the RRM domain of hnRNP, altering its structure and promoting its RNA-binding ability. This finding highlights the direct molecular interaction between CQDs and plant proteins, underscoring their role in influencing biochemical pathways. Key challenges include scaling CQD synthesis and addressing environmental fate (e.g., persistence in soil). Additional proteomics and single-cell RNA-seq analyses could unravel mechanistic gaps. Finally, field trials must validate antiviral efficacy of CQDs in important crops like cotton, where begomoviruses cause catastrophic losses. These findings open the way for further research, discovery, and application of nanotechnology-based solutions for effective, sustainable, and eco-friendly management of plant viral diseases.

Dandan Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ali Kamran: Visualization, Data curation. Zifu He: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Yafei Tang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiaoman She: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
